In Arabic phonology, the phoneme ʿayn (ع) is a voiced pharyngeal fricative with no direct equivalent in European languages. Its transliteration as ʿ (with a left-pointing superscript comma) follows the Library of Congress ALA-LC system. Similarly, hamza (ء) represents a glottal stop and is transliterated as ʾ. Terms like Muḥammad, Allāh, and al-Qurʾān use macrons over long vowels (ā, ū, ī) to distinguish them from short counterparts (a, u, i).
